mission.title: Install and Configure Kcp on Kubernetes
mission.description: kcp is a Kubernetes-like control plane designed for managing multiple isolated clusters, known as workspaces. It enables API service providers to centrally offer APIs and facilitates easy API consumption for users within their workspaces. Installing kcp allows you to leverage its capabilities for multi-tenant applications and services.

steps.title: Add the kcp Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official kcp Helm repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add kcp https://kcp-dev.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install kcp using Helm
steps.description:
Install kcp in a new namespace called 'kcp-system':
```bash
helm install kcp kcp/kcp --namespace kcp-system --create-namespace --version v0.30.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the kcp pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kcp-system
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for kcp
steps.description:
Create a ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding for kcp:
```bash
kubectl create clusterrole kcp-admin --verb=* --resource=* 

kubectl create clusterrolebinding kcp-admin-binding --clusterrole=kcp-admin --serviceaccount=kcp-system:kcp
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of kcp will show the kcp pods running in the 'kcp-system' namespace. You will also have configured resource limits and RBAC for the kcp deployment, ensuring it operates efficiently and securely.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kcp https://kcp-dev.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install kcp kcp/kcp --namespace kcp-system --create-namespace --version v0.30.0

kubectl edit deployment kcp --namespace kcp-system
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   requests:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
#   limits:
#     cpu: "1"
#     memory: "1Gi"

kubectl create clusterrole kcp-admin --verb=* --resource=* 

kubectl create clusterrolebinding kcp-admin-binding --clusterrole=kcp-admin --serviceaccount=kcp-system:kcp

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the kcp release from the kcp-system namespace using Helm to remove the deployment and associated resources.
```bash
helm uninstall kcp --namespace kcp-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation.
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kcp-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the kcp-system namespace and verify that it has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kcp-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and resources in the kcp-system namespace.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kcp-system -o yaml > kcp-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the kcp release to the latest version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kcp kcp/kcp --namespace kcp-system --version v0.31.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the kcp pods are running the new version after the upgrade.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kcp-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If kcp pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that might indicate the cause.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kcp-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during upgrade, ensure that the specified version is available in the Helm repository.
```bash
helm search repo kcp/kcp --versions
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you face permission issues, verify that the ClusterRole and ClusterRoleBinding are correctly set up for the kcp service account.
```bash
kubectl get clusterrolebindings | grep kcp-admin-binding
```

troubleshooting.title: Namespace not found error
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive a namespace not found error, ensure that the kcp-system namespace exists and is correctly referenced in your commands.
```bash
kubectl get namespaces
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
